general.title: General
general.description: The R66 is a five-place, single main rotor, single engine helicopter with skid-type landing gear. Primary fuselage structure is welded steel tubing and riveted aluminum sheet. The tailcone is a monocoque aluminum structure. The engine is located aft of the main baggage compartment and isolated by firewalls.
key_points: Five-place, single main rotor, single engine helicopter
Primary fuselage: welded steel tubing and riveted aluminum sheet
Tailcone: monocoque aluminum skin structure
Engine compartment isolated by firewalls fore and above
Four cabin doors are removable
Fiberglass and thermoplastics used in secondary cabin structure and fairings

rotor_systems.title: Rotor Systems
rotor_systems.description: The main rotor has two all-metal blades mounted to the hub by coning hinges, with the hub attached to the shaft via a teeter hinge. The tail rotor has two all-metal blades with a teetering hub and fixed coning angle. Main rotor blades feature a thick stainless steel spar with bonded aluminum skins.
key_points: Main rotor: two all-metal blades with coning and teeter hinges using self-lubricated bearings
Droop stops provide teeter hinge friction restraint during start/stop
Pitch change bearings enclosed in oil-filled housing sealed with elastomeric boot
Stainless steel leading edge spar resistant to corrosion and erosion
Aluminum skins bonded to spar approximately one inch aft of leading edge
Blades must be refinished if paint erodes to bare metal at skin-to-spar bond line
Tail rotor: two all-metal blades, teetering hub, fixed coning angle
Tail rotor teeter hinge bearings are elastomeric; pitch change bearings have self-lubricated liners

specifications.notes: Approximately 1 inch aft of leading edge

drive_system.title: Drive System
drive_system.description: The engine is mounted at 37° nose-up. A sprag-type overrunning clutch mates to the engine PTO shaft and connects via flexible couplings to the main gearbox. Two ring-and-pinion spiral bevel gearsets reduce speed to tail rotor driveline RPM and then to main rotor RPM. The tail rotor driveline runs aft through the tailcone with a tail gearbox containing a 90° spiral-bevel gearset. The main gearbox is pressure lubricated and also drives the hydraulic pump.
key_points: Engine mounted at 37° nose-up attitude
Sprag-type overrunning clutch mates directly to engine PTO shaft
Two ring-and-pinion spiral bevel gearsets in main gearbox
Tail rotor driveshaft has support bearing at front and damper bearing approximately one-third aft
Cooling fan mounted to intermediate shaft
Tail gearbox: single 90° splash-lubricated spiral-bevel gearset
Main gearbox is pressure lubricated with airframe-mounted filter and oil cooler
Main gearbox drives the flight control hydraulic pump
components: Ring and pinion spiral bevel gearsets (2)

specifications.item: Damper bearing position on tail driveshaft
specifications.notes: Approximately one-third of the way aft on the shaft

powerplant_installation.title: Powerplant Installation
powerplant_installation.description: The R66 is powered by a single Rolls-Royce 250-C300/A1 (RR300) free-turbine turboshaft engine. Induction air enters through upper fuselage cowl openings into a plenum with a radial-flow air filter. If the filter blocks, spring-loaded bypass doors open and the AIR FILTER annunciator illuminates. A temperature switch on the firewall detects engine compartment fire.
key_points: Engine: Rolls-Royce 250-C300/A1 (RR300) free-turbine turboshaft
Direct-drive squirrel-cage cooling fan on intermediate shaft
Standard induction air filter: radial-flow foam element
Optional high-efficiency pleated media filter with sight gauge in upper left cowl door
High-efficiency filter recommended for dusty conditions
Spring-loaded bypass doors open if filter blocks; AIR FILTER annunciator illuminates
Engine compartment fire detected by temperature switch on firewall; ENGINE FIRE annunciator illuminates
Power assurance checks recommended to detect engine deterioration or filter blockage

flight_controls.title: Flight Controls
flight_controls.description: Dual controls are standard. All primary controls use push-pull tubes and bellcranks with sealed or self-lubricated bearings. The cyclic is center-mounted with adjustable grip angle on later aircraft. The collective has a twist grip throttle. Right-side pedals are adjustable to three positions; left-side pedals are fixed (optional shorter pedals available).
key_points: All primary controls actuated through push-pull tubes and bellcranks
Bearings are sealed ball bearings or self-lubricated liners
Center-mounted cyclic with left/right grips on cross tube
Pilot cyclic grip angle adjustable fore/aft on later aircraft
Collective twist grip provides engine fuel control input
Raising/lowering collective provides power turbine governor inputs via interconnecting linkage
Right-side pedals adjustable to three positions via quick-release pin
Left-side pedals not adjustable; optional shorter pedals available (F755-9 and -10)
Pilots must verify full control travel prior to flight
components: Cyclic grip (adjustable angle on later aircraft)

specifications.notes: Robinson F755-9 and F755-10 for shorter pilots

removable_flight_controls.title: Removable Flight Controls
removable_flight_controls.description: The left seat pilot controls (cyclic grip, collective, and tail rotor pedals) can be removed and installed by maintenance personnel or pilots. Specific procedures involving quick-release pins, locking pins, and balance spring management must be followed to avoid damage.
key_points: Left cyclic grip removed via quick-release pin; cross tube must be rotated clockwise one turn to wind balance spring
Later aircraft have knurled ring near quick-release pin to eliminate freeplay; must be loose to remove pin
Overrotating cyclic cross tube in either direction will damage balance spring
Plastic cap should be placed on exposed cyclic cross tube after grip removal
Collective removed by depressing locking pins and pulling forward; both locking pins must be fully engaged when installed
Pedals removed by depressing locking pin, twisting counterclockwise, and pulling up; floor cover available when removed

hydraulic_system.title: Hydraulic System
hydraulic_system.description: A hydraulically-boosted flight control system eliminates cyclic and collective feedback forces. The system consists of a pump driven by the main gearbox, three servos connected to the swashplate push-pull tubes, a reservoir with filter and pressure relief valve, and a pilot-controlled solenoid pressure shut-off valve. Normal operating pressure is 450–500 psi.
key_points: Eliminates cyclic and collective feedback forces
Pump mounted on and driven by main gearbox
Three servos, one per swashplate push-pull tube
Reservoir at aft end of main gearbox includes filter, pressure relief valve, and pressure shut-off valve
Normal operating pressure: 450–500 psi
Sight glass for preflight fluid level check accessible via left cowl door
Vented filler cap on top of reservoir
Pressure shut-off valve solenoid-actuated via HYD switch on pilot's cyclic grip
Switch should be left ON during shutdown and startup except during hydraulic check
Electrical power required to switch hydraulics OFF; pulling HYD circuit breaker disables switch but does not turn off hydraulics
Without hydraulic pressure, large collective input force required and collective inputs cause longitudinal cyclic forces
Servos have irreversible feature; any cyclic input will cause collective to lower

control_friction_adjustment.title: Control Friction Adjustment
control_friction_adjustment.description: Cyclic and collective controls have adjustable friction devices. The collective friction lever is near the aft end of the pilot's collective; actuated aft to increase friction. The cyclic friction knob is left of the cyclic stick; clockwise applies friction to both longitudinal and lateral axes. Pedals have no friction device but use an elastomeric trim spring for left pedal force balance.
key_points: Collective friction lever located near aft end of pilot's collective; aft increases friction, forward releases
Cyclic friction knob located left of cyclic stick; clockwise applies friction to both axes
Excessive friction may make helicopter difficult to control
Pedals have no friction device
Elastomeric trim spring provides left pedal force to balance feedback forces in flight

engine_controls.title: Engine Controls
engine_controls.description: Twist grip throttle controls on each collective stick actuate the engine fuel control via push-pull cable. The throttle is set either fully open or fully closed (idle). A hydromechanical governor maintains 100% N2 RPM when throttle is open, with collective-governor linkage to anticipate power changes. A beep switch trims governor RPM at approximately 1% per 2 seconds.
key_points: Twist grip throttle on each collective; interconnected, actuate fuel control via push-pull cable
Throttle is normally set fully open or fully closed (idle), not used for modulation
Hydromechanical governor maintains 100% engine output shaft RPM at full throttle
Collective-to-governor linkage anticipates changing power demands
Governor may not prevent over/under-speed from aggressive maneuvers or rapid power changes
Beep switch (momentary toggle on collective) trims governor RPM ~1% per 2 seconds
Beep switch controls actuator adjusting collective-to-governor linkage
Other controls: push-pull fuel cutoff on console face, start button on pilot's collective, key-type igniter switch, anti-ice switch
components: Twist grip throttle (pilot and copilot collective)

specifications.notes: Holding beep switch up or down

engine_anti_ice.title: Engine Anti-Ice
engine_anti_ice.description: A solenoid-actuated valve controlled by the anti-ice switch routes hot compressor discharge air to the compressor nose bearing support to prevent ice formation. The green ANTI-ICE annunciator activates via a pressure switch in the anti-ice air line when the system is ON and functioning. The system is fail-safe ON (electrical power required to turn it OFF). Performance degradation occurs with anti-ice ON.
key_points: Solenoid-actuated valve routes hot compressor discharge air to compressor nose bearing support
Green ANTI-ICE annunciator confirms system is ON and functioning via pressure switch
System is fail-safe ON; electrical power to solenoid required to switch OFF
Performance degradation occurs with anti-ice ON (see Section 5)
Must be ON for operation in certain icing conditions per Section 2

starter_and_ignition_system.title: Starter and Ignition System
starter_and_ignition_system.description: A single starter-generator serves both starting and electrical generation. A Generator Control Unit (GCU) manages the sequence: during start the starter latches on automatically until N1 reaches 58%, then switches out of start mode. The igniter fires during start and can be reactivated in flight via the start button. The engine can be motored without ignition for compressor wash.
key_points: Single starter-generator for both starting and generation
GCU latches starter on until N1 reaches 58% RPM; pilot need not hold start button
Above 58% N1, GCU automatically switches out of start mode
Generator switch should not be switched ON until idle RPM stabilizes
With igniter key in enable: normal start sequence with igniter firing
Above 58% N1, igniter fires while start button is held; GEN annunciator illuminates momentarily
Start button can be depressed in flight to activate igniter
With igniter OFF: engine can be motored without starter latching or igniter firing (for compressor wash)
If igniter switched OFF during start, starter disengages
Do not engage starter with generator circuit breaker pulled
Start button active when battery switch is ON, even with igniter OFF
Rotor brake engaged after shutdown disables start button

fuel_system.title: Fuel System
fuel_system.description: A single bladder-type crash-resistant fuel cell supplies fuel by gravity flow to the engine. The cell has vent fittings with rollover valves, a fuel valve at the firewall, and a finger strainer at the outlet. Float switches activate LOW FUEL (~5 gallons) and <12 GAL FUEL (~12 gallons) annunciators. The engine has a fuel pump with inlet filter; FUEL FILTER annunciator illuminates on contamination.
key_points: Single bladder-type crash-resistant fuel cell, gravity feed
LOW FUEL annunciator: ~5 gallons usable remaining (float switch, independent of quantity sender)
<12 GAL FUEL annunciator: ~12 gallons usable remaining (later aircraft); illuminates ~15–20 min before LOW FUEL at moderate/high power
<12 GAL FUEL accuracy affected by pitch/roll attitude and CG; may vary by up to 4 gallons at CG limits
Vent fittings interconnected, vented through mast fairing risers; rollover valves prevent leakage
Fuel valve on forward firewall controlled by push-pull cable at base of pilot's collective
Engine fuel pump assembly with inlet filter; FUEL FILTER annunciator on excessive pressure drop
Fuel filler cap under cowl door
Single sump drain accessible via left cowl door; glass sample tube provided on later aircraft

specifications.notes: Approximate; later aircraft only; accuracy affected by CG/attitude, may vary up to 4 gallons

specifications.item: Time from <12 GAL to LOW FUEL annunciation
specifications.notes: Approximately 15–20 minutes at moderate to high power

electrical_system.title: Electrical System
electrical_system.description: A 28-volt DC system powered by a sealed lead-acid battery and starter-generator controlled by a GCU. The battery switch controls the battery relay. An avionics switch controls power to avionics. An ammeter indicates total generator output and a digital voltmeter reads main bus voltage. Normal bus voltage is 27.3–28.7 V with generator ON.
key_points: 28-volt DC electrical system
Sealed lead-acid battery located in left side of baggage compartment
Battery switch controls battery relay; bypass wire (fuse-protected) powers tachometers and clock with battery switch OFF
Avionics switch allows single-switch shutdown of avionics; protects against voltage spikes when generator switched ON
Ammeter indicates total generator output
Digital voltmeter reads main bus voltage; incorporated in OAT indicator
Normal bus voltage: 27.3–28.7 V with generator ON
GCU in start mode below 58% N1; switches to generate mode above 58% N1 automatically
Generator switch should normally be OFF for starting
If GEN annunciator illuminates in flight: turn off non-essential equipment, switch generator to reset then ON; if remains, land as soon as practical
GEN light may indicate broken generator shaft; re-start not possible after shutdown if shaft is broken
Inflight reset of circuit breakers not recommended
Circuit breaker panel on ledge forward of left front seat; breakers marked with function and amperage

lighting_system.title: Lighting System
lighting_system.description: The R66 has a red anti-collision light on the tailcone, position lights on cabin sides and tail, instrument panel lighting (post lights and top windshield light) active with nav lights switch, a dimmer knob, a swivel map light with high/low settings, and two HID landing lights (wide-angle and narrow-beam) in the nose controlled from the cyclic center post. An optional flashing light may be added to the tailcone.
key_points: Red anti-collision light on tailcone; controlled by strobe switch
Position lights on each side of cabin and in tail; controlled by nav lights switch
Instrument panel lit by top windshield light and post lights; active when nav lights ON; dimmed via knob above nav lights switch
Overhead map light on swivel; high and low settings; not connected to dimmer; can serve as emergency panel lighting
Additional cabin light aft of map light; not on dimmer circuit
Two HID landing lights in nose: one wide-angle, one narrow-beam; single switch on cyclic center post
Continuous operation of landing and position lights recommended for collision avoidance
Optional additional flashing light on tailcone; controlled by extra strobe switch position (earlier) or separate switch (later)
components: HID landing lights (2: wide-angle and narrow-beam)

external_power_receptacle.title: External Power Receptacle
external_power_receptacle.description: A 28-volt SAE AS35061-style external power receptacle is provided. On earlier aircraft it is inside the right engine cowl door; on later aircraft it is in the cabin outboard of the pilot's seat. When the battery switch is ON, both the external power relay and battery relay close to connect external power to the system. Reverse polarity protection is provided. Battery charging provisions exist with battery switch off.
key_points: 28-volt SAE AS35061-style receptacle
Earlier aircraft: inside right engine cowl door
Later aircraft: in cabin outboard of pilot's seat
Battery switch ON causes external power relay and battery relay to both close
External power relay will not close if reverse polarity detected
Battery can be charged with battery switch OFF per Section 8 procedures

instrument_panel.title: Instrument Panel
instrument_panel.description: The standard instrument panel includes flight and engine instruments, engine monitoring gauges, clock, ammeter, OAT/voltmeter, and hourmeters. Standard primary instruments are airspeed indicator, N2/rotor dual tachometer, altimeter, torquemeter, and magnetic compass. Engine gauges include N1 tachometer, MGT, oil pressure, oil temperature, and fuel quantity. Both hourmeters require main gearbox oil pressure; the outboard hourmeter is collective-activated.
key_points: Standard primary instruments: airspeed, N2/rotor tachometer, altimeter, torquemeter, magnetic compass
Engine gauges: N1 tachometer, MGT, oil pressure, oil temperature, fuel quantity
Also standard: clock, ammeter, OAT/voltmeter, hourmeter
Additional outboard hourmeter (collective-activated) for time in service
Both hourmeters require main gearbox oil pressure to activate
Exact panel configuration varies with optional equipment and manufacture date
Optional avionics supplements (PFD/MFD) described in Section 9

annunciator_panel.title: Annunciator Panel
annunciator_panel.description: The annunciator panel is at the top of the main instrument panel and illuminates segments to indicate caution or warning conditions. Segments cover chip detection in gearboxes, main rotor gearbox temperature/pressure, engine fire, engine oil pressure, anti-ice status, generator status, fuel levels, fuel filter, low RPM, cowl door status, air filter bypass, hydraulic shutoff, and EMU status.
key_points: CHIP segments: illuminated by magnetic chip detectors in each gearbox; engine gearbox has two detectors
MR TEMP/PRESS: illuminated by temperature or oil pressure switch at main gearbox
ENGINE FIRE: illuminated by temperature switch in engine compartment
ENGINE OIL: activated by pressure switch independent of oil pressure sender
ANTI ICE (green): pressure switch in anti-ice air line confirms system ON and functioning
GEN: illuminates when generator disconnected from main bus
LOW FUEL: float switch independent of fuel quantity sender; ~5 gallons
<12 GAL FUEL: second float switch on later aircraft; ~12 gallons
FUEL FILTER: pressure switch detects excessive pressure drop across fuel filter
LOW RPM: sensor at aft end of main gearbox; horn sounds simultaneously when rotor below 95% RPM
COWL DOOR: fuel filler or baggage door not closed; later aircraft also engine cowl doors
AIR FILTER: bypass doors open due to blocked filter
HYD: hydraulic system switched off (later aircraft)
EMU segment: steady=normal, slow flash (2s)=fault, fast flash (4/s)=exceedance; only illuminates when test button depressed
Test button illuminates all segments and ROTOR BRAKE light; LOW FUEL/<12 GAL have ~2 second delay
If LOW FUEL illuminates immediately on test, fault indicated
components: CHIP detector segments (main gearbox, tail gearbox, engine gearbox x2)

specifications.notes: Horn muted when collective fully down

specifications.item: LOW FUEL/<12 GAL test button delay
specifications.notes: Time delay prevents false indication from sloshing fuel

specifications.item: EMU self-test time after battery ON
specifications.notes: Before EMU segment will illuminate on test

audio_alerts.title: Audio Alerts
audio_alerts.description: All R66s have a low-RPM horn sounding when rotor RPM is below 95% (muted with collective fully down). Later aircraft add audio alerts in headsets for high rotor RPM (warble tone above 105%) and high engine torque/MGT (beeping tone). Beep rate varies with severity: 4 beeps/sec for torque >100% or MGT >782°C, 12 beeps/sec for torque >108% or any high MGT, and 12 beeps/sec during start if MGT >860°C.
key_points: Low-RPM horn: below 95% rotor RPM; muted when collective fully down
Earlier aircraft: horn via speakers in instrument console side
Later aircraft: horn via tone generator through crew headsets
High RPM alert (later aircraft): warble tone (high/low) above 105% rotor RPM
High torque/MGT alert (later aircraft): beeping tone
Torque >100% or MGT >782°C: 4 beeps/second
Torque >108%: 12 beeps/second
MGT >782°C: 12 beeps/second
Hot start indicator: 12 beeps/second during start if MGT >860°C
Test buttons on instrument panel for high RPM and high torque/MGT tones
High torque/MGT test: 4 beeps/sec for 2 seconds then 12 beeps/sec
Additional alerts possible from optional equipment (terrain, traffic, autopilot)

specifications.item: High torque fast beep threshold (12 beeps/sec)

audio_system.title: Audio System
audio_system.description: A five-place audio system is standard with an audio control panel for communications radios, intercom, and external audio input. Headset jacks are in the ceiling near each seat. Pilot and copilot intercom/transmit are controlled by two-detent cyclic trigger switches. Additional intercom buttons are at rear seats and left forward floor or seat support. Music input jack is on the circuit breaker panel.
key_points: Five-place audio system standard
Audio control panel controls comm radios, intercom, and music/external audio
Multiple audio panel manufacturers offered; consult manufacturer's manual
Headset jacks in ceiling near each seat
Cyclic trigger: first detent = intercom, second detent = transmit
Additional intercom buttons on forward side of rear seats and left forward floor/seat support
Intercom may be voice-activated
External audio jack on circuit breaker panel; muted during radio communication in most settings
components: Cyclic trigger switches (pilot and copilot, two-detent)
Left forward floor/seat support intercom button
External audio input jack (circuit breaker panel)

optional_avionics.title: Optional Avionics
optional_avionics.description: A wide range of optional avionics are available. All aircraft are delivered with manufacturers' operating manuals for installed equipment. Complex options such as PFDs/MFDs are described in the Optional Avionics Supplement in Section 9.
key_points: Wide range of optional avionics available
Manufacturers' operating manuals supplied with each installed piece of equipment
PFD/MFD options detailed in Optional Avionics Supplement, Section 9

dual_tachometer.title: Dual Tachometer
dual_tachometer.description: An electronic N2 and rotor dual tachometer is standard. N2 signal comes from a transducer on the engine gearbox; rotor signal from magnetic senders at the main gearbox tail rotor driveline yoke. Each tachometer has its own circuit breaker. The tachometer bus is powered from the avionics bus or via a battery relay bypass circuit active whenever the main rotor gearbox has oil pressure (i.e., rotors turning).
key_points: Electronic N2 and rotor dual tachometer standard
N2 signal: transducer on engine gearbox
Rotor signal: magnetic senders at main gearbox tail rotor driveline yoke
Each tachometer on separate circuit with own circuit breaker
Tachometer bus powered from avionics bus or battery relay bypass circuit
Bypass circuit active whenever main rotor gearbox has oil pressure (rotors turning), even if avionics bus unpowered
Bypass circuit disconnected from tachometer bus when annunciator test button depressed
Pressing test button while running confirms tachometers receiving power from avionics bus

pitot_static_system.title: Pitot-Static System
pitot_static_system.description: The pitot-static system supplies air pressure to the airspeed indicator, altimeter, and vertical speed indicator. The pitot tube is on the leading edge of the mast fairing. Static sources are on each side of the cabin aft of the rear doors. Water can be drained from lines via plastic drain plugs accessible through the aft underside inspection panel.
key_points: Supplies airspeed indicator, altimeter, and vertical speed indicator
Pitot tube: leading edge of mast fairing
Static sources: each side of cabin aft of rear doors (dual)
Water drain plugs accessible through aft inspection panel on underside of cabin
Draining required only if airspeed or altimeter appears erratic
Pitot and static sources should be inspected frequently for bugs or obstructions
components: Static ports (2, cabin sides aft of rear doors)

engine_monitoring_unit.title: Engine Monitoring Unit
engine_monitoring_unit.description: The EMU is a digital recording device mounted behind the right rear seatback panel. It continuously monitors N1, N2, engine torque, and MGT, and records exceedances of Rolls-Royce engine limits. It also records start cycles when N1 exceeds 30% and MGT is at least 343°C. EMU data can be downloaded to a computer. Status is shown only when the annunciator test button is held.
key_points: Digital recording device behind right rear seatback panel
Monitors continuously: N1, N2, engine torque, MGT
Records exceedances of Rolls-Royce engine limits
Records start cycle when N1 >30% and MGT ≥343°C
EMU data downloadable to computer with appropriate software
EMU segment only illuminates while annunciator test button held
Requires ~10 seconds self-test after battery ON before ready
Steady illumination = normal; slow flash (once/2 sec) or no illumination = fault; fast flash (4/sec) = exceedance
Fault or exceedance must be investigated and reset by qualified mechanic before next flight
EMU is a maintenance aid; pilot responsible for reporting exceedances; operator responsible for start/time records

specifications.item: EMU self-test duration after battery ON

cabin_heating_and_ventilation.title: Cabin Heating and Ventilation
cabin_heating_and_ventilation.description: Fresh air vents are in each door and in the nose. Door vents are controlled by knobs; for maximum ventilation, open wide in hover but only one inch or less in cruise. The nose fresh air inlet (console face pull handle) directs air along the windshield inside for defogging. Cabin heat uses engine compressor bleed air routed to footwell outlets, controlled by a push-pull cable knob left of the cyclic. Performance degradation occurs with heat ON.
key_points: Door vents: open/close by knob; rotating knob seals and locks; max open in hover, ≤1 inch in cruise
Nose fresh air inlet: pull handle on console face; rotating knob clockwise locks position
Nose air directed along inside of windshield for defogging and ventilation
Cabin heat uses engine compressor bleed air
Heat outlets forward of tail rotor pedals and in rear footwells
Heater control knob left of cyclic stick; push-pull cable to valve in aft control tunnel
Performance degradation with cabin heat ON (see Section 5)
In case of engine fire, cabin heat should be turned OFF
components: Door fresh air vents (with locking knobs)
Cabin heat outlets (forward footwell and rear footwells)

specifications.item: Door vent max opening in cruise

seats_belts_and_baggage.title: Seats, Belts, and Baggage
seats_belts_and_baggage.description: Seats are not adjustable but right-side pedals are. A removable back cushion positions shorter pilots forward. Optional heated seat bottoms and backrests for front and outboard rear seats operate only with generator ON. Each seat has a combined lap belt and inertia reel shoulder strap. Optional five-point harnesses available for front seats. The main baggage compartment has a large right-side door with annunciator, interior light, and tie-down anchors. Under-seat compartments with maximum fill lines exist under all seats except the center rear.
key_points: Seats not adjustable; removable back cushion aids shorter pilots
Optional seat heaters (front and outboard rear) with high/low settings; operate only with generator ON
Seat heater switches above right-side collective boot
Each seat: lap belt with inertia reel shoulder strap (locks on sudden movement)
Optional five-point harnesses for front seats; buckle must not interfere with cyclic at aft position
Main baggage compartment: right-side door, COWL DOOR annunciator warns if unlatched, interior light on with battery switch ON, tie-down anchors
Under-seat compartments under all seats except center rear; do not load above max fill lines (crush space required)
Optional ceiling anchor loops for safety tether during doors-off operation
components: Seat heaters (optional, front and outboard rear)

specifications.notes: Low and high; generator switch must be ON

landing_gear.title: Landing Gear
landing_gear.description: Skid-type landing gear with elastic energy absorption for most hard landings. In extremely hard landings, struts hinge up and outward as the crosstube yields permanently to absorb impact. Crosstube replacement required if tail skid is within 38 inches (30 inches for extended gear) of ground when sitting empty on level pavement. Aerodynamic fairings are fitted to struts. Abrasion-resistant wear shoes are on the skids and should be replaced when thinnest wear point is less than 0.05 inches.
key_points: Skid-type landing gear
Most hard landings absorbed elastically
Extreme hard landing: struts hinge up/outward; crosstube yields to absorb impact
Slight crosstube yielding acceptable; replacement required if tail skid ≤38 inches from ground (empty, level pavement)
Extended gear: replacement threshold is ≤30 inches
Four aerodynamic fairings on struts; helicopter approved to fly with or without fairings
Abrasion-resistant wear shoes on skid bottoms; replace when thinnest point <0.05 inches (1.3 mm)
Wear shoes should be inspected periodically, especially after autorotation landings
Optional mirrors near forward end of one or both skid tubes; friction ball joint adjustable

specifications.notes: Tail skid height from ground, empty on level pavement
Tail skid height from ground, empty on level pavement

rotor_brake.title: Rotor Brake
rotor_brake.description: The rotor brake is mounted on the aft end of the main gearbox and actuated by a cable connected to a pull handle on the cabin ceiling. It is used to stop the rotor after engine shutdown and can be used as a parking brake. The brake must be released before starting; when engaged, the starter is disabled. At least one minute must elapse after engine shutdown before applying the brake, and force must be moderate (not stopping rotor in less than 10 seconds) to avoid damage.
key_points: Mounted on aft end of main gearbox
Pull handle on cabin ceiling; cable actuated
Use procedure: wait at least 1 minute after fuel cutoff, then pull handle with ~10 lb force
After rotor stops, bead chain hooks in bracket slot to use as parking brake
Annunciator light near igniter switch illuminates when brake engaged
Brake must be released before starting engine
When brake engaged, starter is disabled
Rotor brake engaged after shutdown disables the start button
Applying brake too soon or stopping rotor in <10 seconds may damage brake shoes and gearbox oil seal

specifications.item: Minimum wait after engine shutdown before applying brake

specifications.notes: Stopping faster may damage brake shoes and gearbox oil seal

ads_b_equipment.title: ADS-B Equipment
ads_b_equipment.description: An ADS-B capable transponder is installed on later aircraft, transmitting GPS position to ATC. ADS-B Out meets 14 CFR §91.227 requirements and operates on 1090 MHz. ADS-B In (optional) can receive traffic and weather from other ADS-B aircraft and ground stations on both 978 MHz and 1090 MHz. ADS-B In requires a suitable display such as a moving map GPS or MFD.
key_points: ADS-B Out transponder installed on later aircraft
Transmits GPS position to supplement radar/transponder information
ADS-B Out meets 14 CFR §91.227 requirements
ADS-B Out frequency: 1090 MHz (also accepted in most countries outside USA)
ADS-B Out operation is mostly automatic; malfunctions annunciated on transponder/GPS
ADS-B Out can be turned off via transponder controls but should not be unless directed by ATC
ADS-B In (optional): receives traffic and weather from ADS-B aircraft and ground stations
ADS-B In receives both US approved frequencies: 978 MHz and 1090 MHz
ADS-B In requires suitable display (moving map GPS or MFD)
components: Moving map GPS or MFD (for ADS-B In display)

specifications.notes: 978 MHz and 1090 MHz (both US approved frequencies)

emergency_locator_transmitter.title: Emergency Locator Transmitter (Optional)
emergency_locator_transmitter.description: The optional ELT consists of a transmitter with internal battery, external antenna, and a remote switch/annunciator. The transmitter is mounted to the upper steel tube frame and accessed through the right-side cowl spring-loaded air intake door. For flight, the transmitter switch should be in ARM and the remote switch in ARMED. The ELT activates automatically on high-G impact. The remote switch also allows manual ON activation and momentary RESET & TEST.
key_points: Transmitter mounted to upper steel tube frame; accessed through right-side cowl air intake door
Remote switch/annunciator located left of cyclic stick
Transmitter switch must be in ARM position for flight
Remote switch must be in ARMED position for flight
Activates automatically on high-G load when both switches set to armed
Red indicator light illuminates when transmitting
Remote switch ON activates transmitter manually (use if emergency landing imminent and time permits)
RESET & TEST (momentary) stops accidental transmission and resets unit; red light extinguishes when reset

accessory_mounts.title: Accessory Mounts (Optional)
accessory_mounts.description: Optional mounting bars forward of pilot's and/or copilot's seat with clamp assemblies compatible with commercially available accessory mounts. Each bar has a 10 lb total weight limit. USB and cigarette-lighter-style power outlets are at the inboard end, protected by the Aux Power circuit breaker and in-line fuses. Wire clamps and a wire pocket are provided.
key_points: Mounting bars forward of pilot's seat, copilot's seat, or both
Clamp assemblies compatible with variety of commercially available mounts
10 lb total weight limit per mounting bar
USB and cigarette-lighter-style power outlets at inboard end of bar
Power outlets protected by Aux Power circuit breaker and in-line fuses; placarded with voltage/current ratings
Wire clamps and pocket for securing excess wire provided
Mounting bar, clamp assembly, and power outlets are approved aircraft type design; attached items are pilot's responsibility
Ensure items are secure and do not interfere with controls or primary field of view

specifications.notes: Total for all items on bar

cockpit_camera.title: Cockpit Camera (Optional)
cockpit_camera.description: An optional ceiling-mounted video camera records 4K video, intercom/comm audio, and GPS position to internal memory and a removable flash drive. Recording starts automatically when battery switches on and stops when off. A 128 GB flash drive records approximately 10 hours; when full, earliest files are overwritten. Video files are sequential 4 GB MP4 files (~25 min each); GPS stored in GPX files.
key_points: Ceiling-mounted video camera
Records 4K video, intercom/comm audio, and GPS position
Records to internal memory (recent video only, not user accessible) and removable flash drive
Recording starts automatically when battery ON, stops when battery OFF
Record and audio switches on front of camera housing allow stopping recording or muting audio
Do not remove flash drive during recording; stop recording first and wait for blue light to stop flashing
Blue flashing light: recording to flash drive; green steady light: powered and normal; amber flashing: internal fault
Video: sequential 4 GB files, ~25 min each, labeled HELICAM_xxxx.MP4
GPS: HELICAM_xxxx.GPX files
128 GB flash drive = ~10 hours of video; earliest file overwritten when full
Flash drives must meet criteria in Cockpit Camera User Guide

cyclic_guard.title: Cyclic Guard (Optional)
cyclic_guard.description: An optional bar extending from the inboard corner of the left front seat to the instrument console, acting as a barrier to prevent inadvertent cyclic interference and providing a hand grip for the left front seat passenger. It can be temporarily lowered to access the under-seat compartment via a silver spring knob at the forward end. Recommended whenever a non-pilot occupies the left front seat.
key_points: Bar from inboard corner of left front seat to instrument console
Acts as barrier to prevent inadvertent cyclic interference
Provides hand grip for passenger's right hand
To lower: pull silver spring knob at forward end; aft end rotates down
To re-secure: lift aft end and spring knob locks back in place
Recommended when non-pilot occupies left front seat
Pilot flying from left seat may find guard contacts right leg; should be removed if objectionable
